DNP-FNP Candidate  ·  George Mason University

Arianna
Castro

MSN, RN  ·  Anticipated Graduation May 2026

I am a nurse, DNP student, educator, and lifelong learner with a passion for compassionate, culturally responsive care. Over the past six years at Inova, I have grown as a nurse and a leader while building a strong foundation in patient-centered care. My background also includes a master's degree in nursing informatics with experience working with the Epic team at Inova to improve workflows within the charting system. I also have experience as a Graduate Teaching Assistant, working at the MAT clinic, where I have supported patients with complex needs. My passion is to make the most of every opportunity with patients and connect with them on a human level. I believe that healing begins with trust, dignity, and understanding. I strive to improve care for the vulnerable and underserved communities. Outside of nursing, I am a mother of two girls, who inspire my commitment to growth, resilience, and meaningful services.

DNP Scholarly Project

Developing Trauma-Informed Care
in Refugee Health

A Six-Week Training for Afghan Refugee Care Through Community Health Worker Integration

A Quality Improvement project implemented at Virginia partner clinics participating in the SASCI grant — developing, implementing, and evaluating a CHW-integrated trauma-informed care training module to improve culturally and linguistically responsive care for Afghan refugees in Virginia.

Guided by the Precede-Proceed Model and Campinha-Bacote's Cultural Competence Framework (ASKED), training was delivered in person and via Google Classroom.

Quality Improvement SASCI Grant CHW Integration ARTIC-10
6

Week structured training curriculum delivered in person and via Google Classroom

10

ARTIC domains assessed pre and post training across all participant groups

100%

Of measured knowledge and skills areas showed improvement following training

Project Purpose & Aims

Develop, implement, and evaluate a CHW-integrated trauma-informed care training module to improve culturally and linguistically responsive care for Afghan refugees in Virginia. Goal #1: Design a trauma-informed training curriculum incorporating CHW expertise and key refugee-care competencies. Goal #2: Improve providers' knowledge, clinical skills, and confidence.

Setting & Population

Afghan refugees resettling in Virginia. Facility: Mason and Partners Refugee Clinic, participating in the Services to Afghan Survivors Impacted by Combat (SASCI) grant. Subjects: clinic staff, nurses, providers, case workers, students, volunteers, and pharmacists.

Methodology & Frameworks

Guided by the Precede-Proceed model — assessing healthcare provider needs and highlighting predisposing knowledge and attitudes. Campinha-Bacote's ASKED model for cultural competence. Tools: Pre/post ARTIC-10 surveys, bi-weekly reflections, and staff engagement tracking.

Future Recommendations

Longitudinal follow-up to assess sustained knowledge retention over 6–12 months. Measure patient outcomes directly linked to CHW TIC trainings. Integrate training into CHW onboarding workflow. Policy advocacy for TIC integration into refugee resettlement programs.

Outcomes & Findings

Project Analysis

ARTIC DomainPrePost
Ability to Implement TIC4.315.14 ↑
TIC Approach Effectiveness4.625.14 ↑
Reactions to Work (Coping)4.234.71 ↑
Long-Term TIC Commitment4.464.71 ↑
Managing TIC Responsibilities4.084.43 ↑
On-the-Job Behavior (Peer)4.384.14 ↓
System-Wide TIC Commitment4.924.57 ↓
Organizational Support4.154.00 ↓
Administrative Support4.003.71 ↓
Support for TIC Implementation4.544.00 ↓
Overall TIC Readiness4.334.24 ↓

Increased Provider Readiness

The culturally tailored training modules increased provider readiness, confidence, and recognition of trauma-related needs among the Afghan refugee population.

Organizational Barriers Identified

While participant engagement and several ARTIC domains improved, post-ARTIC scores decreased, particularly in organizational and administrative support, suggesting that long-term success will require stronger leadership engagement and system-level infrastructure.

Provider vs. Organizational Readiness

The difference between provider and organizational readiness came to light after implementation, revealing that providers' knowledge, confidence, and understanding improved, but low scores in administrative and organizational support indicated that education alone is not enough to sustain practice change.

All Knowledge Areas Improved

Increased Knowledge and Skills: All ten areas showed significant improvement after the training, validating the effectiveness of the culturally tailored, CHW-integrated curriculum.

Target Audience

Afghan Refugees &
Vulnerable Populations

Afghan refugees settling in Virginia face significant cultural, linguistic, and healthcare barriers; they also experience trauma through three critical phases, which the WHO framework conceptualizes as the pre-migration, migration, and post-migration journey.

Instead of focusing solely on symptoms or behaviors, trauma-informed care encourages us to consider the patient's lived experiences and respond in ways that promote safety, dignity, and healing. At MAP, this framework shapes how we communicate, build trust, and involve patients in their care. For Afghan refugees, this is especially important because trauma may be connected to war, displacement, loss, and resettlement stress.

Community Health Workers strengthen this framework by helping bridge cultural differences, build trust, address language barriers, and expand access to resources.

The findings stressed that lasting implementation requires system-level support, leadership engagement, and resources to enforce trauma-informed practices within the clinic. Overall, the process contributed to cultural humility, leadership, and evidence-based practice skills while underscoring the value of advocating for vulnerable populations and promoting healthcare systems that are culturally responsive, equitable, and trauma-informed.

Personal Brand Story

My nursing career began in the Trauma/neuro ICU, where I developed hands-on experience, clinical judgment, and confidence that have shaped my foundation as a nurse. Caring for patients with traumatic and life-altering conditions deepened my compassion and empathy, and taught me the dynamic of patient-and-family centered care. This experience has helped me grow in my communication abilities, provide comfort-focused care, and be there for patients and families during some of their most vulnerable moments.

Not only did I grow while implementing my project, but my GTA work has also played a major role in shaping my understanding of all three frameworks. Working as a GTA at the MAT clinic and Chronic Health Panel gave me the opportunity to support student learning while strengthening my own understanding of complex patient care needs. The ability to connect evidence-based knowledge to real clinical application has evolved my patient care plans and equipped me with sound knowledge for future patients. Working with such vulnerable populations, I learned and applied what person-centered care meant to me, and I was able to look beyond the diagnosis to understand patients' lived experiences, their barriers, and their goals.

The integration of population health and health equity has broadened as I gain more experience in dealing with patients who face chronic illness or substance abuse challenges and significant social determinants of health. Having the knowledge to highlight barriers like stigma, access to care, finances, and mental health have created a softer tone in caring for patients. I take pride in how I care for patients as a whole and acknowledge the broader context that affects health outcomes. Interprofessional collaboration is a critical piece in ensuring that the patient's needs are met at every level.

My time at George Mason University and at the MAT clinic has given me the opportunity to form strong connections with my mentors, providers at the clinic, and the community, which all played an important role in my journey.

Key Nursing Documents
Shaping My Journey

First, the AACN Essentials served as a foundation for not only professional development but nursing education. The Essentials highlight the competencies needed for nurses to deliver high-quality care through all levels of practice. The ten domains is what challenges nurses to become scholars, leaders, collaborators, and lead change within broken healthcare systems. At the end of my DNP journey, I now understand that an advanced nurse is not only about treating the illness but also concentrating on what can increase patients' outcomes such as addressing inequities, improving systems, and leading meaningful change.

The Future of Nursing 2020–2030 Report focuses on the actions nurses can help advance health equity within the coming decade. Nurses continue to play a major role in addressing social determinants of health, improving access to care, reducing disparities, and responding to challenges and the ever-changing needs within communities. It also emphasizes the need for nurses to be prepared in the ten essentials mentioned in the AACN Essentials. Nurses must be supported and encouraged to practice to the full extent of their training and education. Healthcare will not be able to improve unless we address larger systems and social conditions that influence health outcomes. After utilizing this framework, I have realized the importance of going above and beyond as a nurse leader and expand my knowledge within my community, healthcare system, and policy.

Healthy People 2030 adds another level of importance by providing national goals and measurable objectives for improving the well-being of populations. This model focuses extensively on health promotion, disease prevention, and social determinants of health. Healthy People 2030 highlights how health is influenced by outside factors such as housing, education, transportation, language barriers, access to nutritious foods, community safety, and social support. Highlighting such factors encourages nurses to think proactively rather than reactively. It is encouraged for nurses to address the root causes of poor health and create an environment in which patients' communities can thrive in.

AACN Essentials Mapping Table

How the AACN Essentials, Future of Nursing Report, and Healthy People 2030 intersect with my DNP Project and GTA role at the MAP Clinic.

AACN Essential Future of Nursing Report Healthy People 2030 Intersections & Resonance Connecting My DNP Journey
Domain 1
Knowledge of nursing practice; enhancing clinical judgment while using EBP.		 Nurses need strong scientific knowledge and evidence-based practice. Asking for nurses to use EBP to address social determinants of health. Interventions must be based on EBP and strong high-quality decision making. Addressing the importance of EBP in population health and quality improvement. Before developing my project, I conducted a systematic research and literature synthesis to validate my project. Building a trauma-informed training framework that is specific to Afghan refugees. Project: I am proficient in domain 1 by justifying the importance of my intervention through evidence-based literature and clinical reasoning. At the Public College of Grand Health rounds, I utilized evidence-based practice to explain the significance of integrating community health workers into vulnerable and underserved communities, with an audience of over 45 people.

GTA: Having the opportunity to be a GTA has strengthened my knowledge of nursing practice and expanding my understanding of long-term complications. This also gives me the opportunity to explain and teach clinical reasoning to nursing students while strengthening my teaching skills.
Domain 2
Person-centered care; respecting the values of other cultures and meeting the needs of patients in a wholistic way.		 Advocating for wholistic culturally competent care. Highlights the importance of addressing social determinants of health and strengthening partnerships within the community. Seeking improvement on the well-being of an individual and creating care plans centered around patient's needs. My DNP project utilizes the gaps within the community and creates bridges for those gaps. Focusing on populations' unmet needs and social determinants of health. Project: Creating and implementing a 6-week training module within the Mason and Partners clinic has enhanced my skills through consistent patient-centered decision making, population-focused thinking, and leadership in practice change. Translating patient needs into a sustainable practice change by implementing trauma-informed care strengthens domain 2 (Person-Centered Care) by aligning services with patients, populations, values, culture, and lived experiences, while achieving measurable outcomes.

GTA: Being a provider and a leader at the Chronic Health Panel positions me to create care plans that are patient centered by recognizing culture, trauma, stigma, and personal values in health decisions. Each clinical rotation I emphasize the importance for students to lead their care with compassion and cultural humility while being a supporter and nurturer for their patients.
Domain 3
Population health; addressing social determinants of health.		 Highlights the importance of addressing social determinants of health and strengthening partnerships within the community. Focusing on improving population health and reduce disparities. Addressing the importance of EBP in population health and quality improvement. The project seeks to meet the needs of Afghan refugees and highlight the unique social determinants of health that they face. This project does not directly involve patients but will create specific safer outcomes to reduce mental health and re-traumatization. Project: My project is designed to change not only at the individual level but also at the community level, and to improve the outcomes served by the MAP clinic. By underscoring patients' social determinants of health, the project enhances providers' clinical decision-making and planning through patient-centered care that identifies barriers, promotes equity, and addresses the root causes of health disparities.

GTA: Being at the MAT and Chronic Health Panel highlights how SDOH can affect patient outcomes. As a holistic provider one must intentionally address SDOH such as lack of transportation, status of insurance, access to food, mental health, and community support. This role has strengthened the work of prevention, access, and coordinated care on a broader population-impact.
Domain 4
Scholarship for the nursing discipline; translating evidence into practice.		 Creating safer systems and care environments. Encourages nurses to be a part of an interdisciplinary team and communicate with all team members. Reducing specific outcomes such as infections and pressure wounds. To meet health needs at a national level there must be a continuum of team collaboration. Before creating the modules, I had to collaborate with clinic leaders, directors, community health workers, and stakeholders to identify what gaps needed to be addressed. This project aims to integrate three clinicals to deliver consistent quality care that is culturally and linguistically competent. Project: Integrating community health workers into the training modules required professional communication, clear role definitions, and accountability for creating sensitive, trauma-informed content that was delivered appropriately. Teaching community health workers the importance of demonstrating a TIC framework allows an easier transition into workflow therefore easier application in the clinic.

GTA: Integration of EBP knowledge into teaching and clinical discussion has been a challenging domain but has strengthen trust from my nursing students and has given me the confidence to be able to articulate current research, guidelines, and best practices such as the utilization of MyPlate, ASCVD risk score, and USPSTF.
Domain 5
Quality and safety; reducing harm and ensuring liability.		 Knowledge of effects of policies and costs to a health system. Highlights the importance of understanding and developing data driven practices. Addressing the importance of EBP in population health and quality improvement. To track outcomes using a system-wide tracking data follows whether goals and objectives were met. The project seeks to meet the needs of Afghan refugees and highlight the unique social determinants of health that they face. Project: Developing a TIC framework within the clinic decreases the chances of providers and other healthcare workers re-traumatizing patients. In the training module there was an exercise on how to de-escalate a situation when a patient is verbally or physically showing any aggression. In addition, developing anonymous surveys to evaluate the effectiveness of the training modules shows leadership that supports participant confidentiality and psychological safety while having honest, high-quality feedback.

GTA: Working with the nursing students I have noticed a gap in charting as a nurse. I have noticed students using AI to write their notes which can be dangerous for the patient and the nurses' license. I take time after each clinic and go over each student's charting and teach them what charting as a nurse should contain.
Domain 6
Interprofessional partnerships; collaborating with other disciplines.		 Encourages nurses to be a part of an interdisciplinary team and communicate with all team members. To meet health needs at a national level there must be a continuum of team collaboration. Using Qualtrics, Google Classroom, and creating QR codes has enhanced my technical abilities. Being a DNP student, I ensure to always maintain professional while working with the healthcare team and with patients. Project: Leading with change to adapt and improve within a clinic created many opportunities to collaborate with a multidisciplinary team. First identifying the level of knowledge on TIC from a community health workers perspective as well as providers' perspective. Working with stakeholders from other clinics to strengthen the interdisciplinary team. Collaborating with CHWs to meet the needs of patients such as cultural brokers, bridge communication barriers, and promoting trust.

GTA: Being in such a vast role allows me to collaborate with multidisciplinary teams such as community health workers, interpreters, pharmacists, social workers, and providers. Both clinics rely on teamwork and collaboration to help support the whole patient.
Domain 7
System-based practices; understanding the effects of policies and costs at a personal and system level.		 Ensuring nurses carry integrity while advocating for patients. Address strategies on how to build stronger public health systems. Developing a learning module for multiple clinics has enhanced my leadership abilities and developed strong skill sets to meet the needs of health care providers and patients. Project: Changing policy and workflow addresses system-level changes. Adding a new workflow to community health workers on-boarding supports the TIC framework and creates sustainability within the clinic. Breaking down an ROI helps stakeholders visualize the costs and benefits of integrating TIC into policy and workflow.

GTA: Understanding access issues, policies, and systems in healthcare, better guide patient care plans to ensure they are cost efficient, easily accessible, and decreases barriers that the patient may face.
Domain 8
Informatics and healthcare technologies; using technology and data to improve health care.		 Understanding and recognizing future challenges healthcare may face and how to address those issues. To improve population health providers must be ethical and hold professional standards. Developing a learning module for multiple clinics has enhanced my leadership abilities and developed strong skill sets to meet the needs of health care providers and patients. Project: Utilizing Google Classroom, developing training modules through PowerPoint, creating surveys through Qualtrics, and creating QR codes is all part of technology supporting the organization, implementation, and evaluation of the project.

INOVA: At my place of work, I am considered a "super user" who teaches other staff on the unit when workflows change in the charting system. I also work with the Epic developing team and collaborate on ways to make charting more efficient on the floor.
Domain 9
Professionalism; being accountable for your actions by having ethical practices.		 Ensuring nurses carry integrity while advocating for patients. To improve population health providers must be ethical and hold professional standards. Developing a workforce that is adaptable and resilient. Being a DNP student, I ensure to always maintain professional while working with the healthcare team and with patients. Developing a learning module for multiple clinics has enhanced my leadership abilities. Project: Professionalism was reflected while implementing this project by emphasizing culturally sensitive, ethical, and accountable care for a vulnerable population. The TIC framework emphasizes the importance of cultural humility, respect, and empathy. Ethical consideration was demonstrated by protecting participant confidentiality alongside ensuring the unique trauma histories of Afghan refugees were respected.

GTA: Being a GTA means being accountable, respectful, and having ethical practice to support both students and patients. Working with a vast population reinforces the importance of being non-judgmental and demonstrating cultural humility. This has strengthened my professional identity as an advanced nurse leader.
Domain 10
Personal, Professional, and Leadership Development; growing and developing a professional leader with confidence and resiliency.		 Understanding and recognizing future challenges healthcare may face and how to address those issues. Developing a workforce that is adaptable and resilient. Developing a learning module for multiple clinics has enhanced my leadership abilities and develop strong skill sets to meet the needs of health care providers and patients. Project: Developing and implementing a new framework to an established clinic with a multidisciplinary team and working with a grant has strengthened my growth as a DNP leader. My confidence, resilience, and adaptability has grown exponentially throughout this process. Both my professional and personal growth has been shaped to a stronger nurse leader, who is committed to improving healthcare outcomes for the underserved and uninsured.

GTA: Fulfilling the role of a GTA fostered leadership development for not only nursing students but for my DNP peers. Teaching, mentoring, and practicing clinical reasoning has strengthen my confidence in ways I could never imagine. This opportunity has encouraged personal growth as well as professional growth through responsibility, resiliency, and adaptability not only throughout my project but as a teacher and a DNP clinical leader.

  • Graduate Teaching Assistant at GMU focusing on underserved populations including primary care, pediatrics, and medicated-assisted treatment for Opioid Use Disorder (MOUD).
